text: The surgeon takes down the lateral attachments to prepare for medial to lateral dissection, elevating the recto-sigma junction in an anterior orientation.

The surgeon performs lateral attachment dissection, searching for the interdigitating sigmoid fossa. The left ureter is located immediately deep to this fossa, and the dissection continues in this area.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter and begins the medial dissection, marking the start of the surgical procedure.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system to tent the recto-sigma junction anteriorly, adjusting the position for optimal visualization and dissection.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to tent the recto-sigma junction anteriorly, positioning it underneath the second arm. This maneuver fans out the mesentery and lifts the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle.
The surgeon lifts the mesosigmoid and rectosigmoid junction, tenting them up to reveal the sacral promontory. The video is paused to highlight the visibility of the sacral promontory.
The surgeon identifies the mesorectum, which appears as a light yellowish tissue, contrasting with the darker yellowish tissue in the surrounding area.
The surgeon jiggles the peritoneum to identify the junction between the retroperitoneum and the mesorectum, preparing to make a medial lateral incision along the identified line.
The surgeon performs pneumodissection into the presacral space, creating a visible plume as the peritoneum is jiggled and an incision is made.
The surgeon initiates the medial lateral dissection, focusing on the mesorectal area, which is visually distinct from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the hypogastric nerves and carefully pushes them down to preserve them. The third robotic arm remains stationary, while the second arm lifts the mesorectum directly anteriorly.
The surgeon inserts the left hand into the surgical field, spreads the fingers, and lifts the hand, demonstrating the poke, spread, and lift maneuver.
The surgeon performs the 'poke, spread, and lift' maneuver, gently manipulating the tissue to avoid tearing the mesentery.
The surgeon navigates into the pre-circular space, searching for the left ureter using a cephalad and lateral approach, while carefully manipulating surrounding tissues.

The surgeon identifies the aerial tissue plane and pushes it down, along with the hypogastric nerves. The left ureter is located, and the surgeon continues the poke, spread, and lift maneuver to manipulate the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a mouse pointer to indicate the area of mesorectal and mesosigmoid dissection, continuing proximally towards the takeoff of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) from the aorta.

The surgeon exchanges the third robotic arm to lift the dissection point, continuing proximal dissection to remove embryological attachments and peel the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) from the retroperitoneum and the aorta.
The surgeon searches for the junction of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and the aorta, aiming to identify the T-shaped takeoff where the aorta forms the base.

The surgeon identifies the T-shaped junction with the IMA as the base, the superior mesenteric artery extending to the right, and the left colic artery extending to the left. The third robotic arm lifts the dissection point, with the aorta visible at the bottom of the screen.

The surgeon uses scissors to carefully dissect and create a window around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, aiming to form a T-shaped configuration. A lymph node near the IMA is cauterized during the process.

The surgeon has created a window around the IMA pedicle, with the aorta visible below and the descending mesocolon above. Areolar tissue attachments are present, and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is visible deep to these structures.

The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is prominently visible in the center of the screen. To the left, the left colic artery is identified, while to the right, the IMA transitions into the superior rectal artery. The surgeon indicates a 2 to 3 centimeter segment of the IMA pedicle and prepares to maneuver around it.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the identified IMA pedicle, completing the dissection around the junction of the IMA and aorta. The surgical field shows the T-shaped configuration of the vessels, with the aorta at the base.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to meticulously divide the vessels, sealing them multiple times before cutting. The edited video omits some of the repetitive sealing actions.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to lift the divided IMA pedicle anteriorly towards the abdominal wall. The video is sped up as the surgeon continues the medial dorsal dissection, pushing down retroperitoneal structures, including the left ureter and Girota's fascia, while progressing laterally.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection, navigating through tissue planes. Occasional bleeding indicates deviation from the correct anatomical plane, highlighting the need for careful adjustment. The patient's thin body habitus is visually apparent during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies a thin plane within the descending mesocolon, ensuring not to dissect too deeply. Blood vessels are directed downward, while the yellow adipose tissue is positioned upward, highlighting the anatomical separation.

The surgeon navigates through the surgical field, encountering a bridging mesenteric vessel that is positioned too deep. The vessel is divided and pushed down to restore the embryological plane, adjusting the depth of dissection within the retroperitoneal area.
The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to poke, spread, and lift, pushing down the retroperitoneal attachments while working laterally to achieve maximum lateral and proximal mobilization.

The surgeon observes the floppy sigmoid descending colon and prepares to use scissors to select a midpoint for further dissection. The previously dissected area reveals the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) visible in the background.

The surgeon discusses the approach to splenic flexure mobilization, indicating the duodenal-jejunal junction on the left of the screen. The potential path for taking down the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is highlighted, moving cephalad after the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) division.
The surgeon inserts scissors to make a full-thickness incision along the mid part of the lateral attachments, targeting the white line to connect with the previously dissected medial plane.
The surgeon addresses the filmy lateral attachments, starting from the midpoint of the sigmoid colon and progressing upwards towards the splenic flexure, without fully mobilizing the flexure.
The surgeon enters the pre-circle space and continues dividing the lateral attachments distally, aiming to reach either the partner reflection or the initial dissection point on the right-hand side.

The third robotic arm enters the surgical field with its tip pointing away, grasping the mesorectum at the recto sigmoid junction. It pinches and pulls the tissue towards the head, providing clear exposure to the pelvis. The surgeon begins a total mesorectal excision (TME) due to the presence of a recto sigmoid tumor.

The surgeon continues the sigmoid dissection, resembling a tumor-specific mesorectal excision (TME), as they enter the presacral space. The video highlights the technique used for TME within this anatomical area.
The surgeon uses their left hand at a right angle to poke at the midline of the mesorectum, lifting it anteriorly in a circular motion to facilitate the TME dissection.
The surgeon ensures the dissection is performed above the hypogastric nerve plexus, visible as filmy structures, while the left hand, off-screen, performs most of the manipulation.

The surgeon uses their left hand at a right angle to poke and push in the midline of the mesorectum, lifting anteriorly towards the abdominal wall and pulling towards the head in a circular motion. This maneuver is repeated consistently to reposition and manipulate the tissue.

The surgeon initiates the TME dissection at the posterior midline, using the left-handed instrument to mature the mesorectal dissection by pushing it to the right, while maintaining control over the instrument's movements.

The surgeon uses the left-handed instrument to apply counter traction by pushing to the right during the left-sided dissection. The dissection begins at the midline and progresses towards the patient's right side. Upon reaching the pelvic sidewall, the surgeon pulls the mesorectum to the left, visibly manipulating the tissue.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the rectum, pulling it away from the pelvic sidewall to avoid creating a hole. The third robotic arm is repositioned to assist in maintaining the rectum's position, ensuring safe dissection depth.
The surgeon completes the posterior dissection and begins to take down the lateral attachments, moving distally towards the tumor. Counter traction is applied to facilitate the dissection process.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to perform dissection below the tattoo mark, ensuring symmetry by replicating the dissection technique on the left and right sides.

The surgeon positions the third robotic arm beneath the second arm to continue the posterior mesorectal dissection. The dissection begins at the posterior midline, progressing to the left and then to the right. The pre-circular venous plexus is visible in the center, which the surgeon carefully avoids.
The surgeon uses a double screen setup with picture-in-picture to position the colonoscope, visually inspecting the distal margin of the tumor to ensure there are no concerns regarding its clearance.

The surgeon completes the right lateral dissection, ensuring the dissection is well below the tattoo marking. The posterior mobilization is extensive, and the division is planned just below the tattoo, although the adenocarcinoma is superficial and not visually detectable.

The endoscopic view shows the lower edge of the tumor, with the tattoo marking visible. The surgeon verifies the position relative to the tattoo, confirming alignment with the gastroenterologist's assessment.

The surgeon scores the mesorectum circumferentially, marking the rectum and mesorectum for precise division. The scoring is visible on the rectum, indicating where the mesorectum and rectum will be divided.

The surgeon performs posterior circumferential scoring on the rectum, creating landmarks to guide the subsequent dissection through the mesorectum, ensuring precise navigation and avoiding excessive skiving.
The surgeon performs posterior mesorectal dissection and mesenteric division using scissors, carefully navigating the anatomical structures.
The surgeon skeletonizes the tissue to expose and identify the superior haremortal artery. Using a bipolar instrument, the artery is divided. The rectum is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and divides the upper hormonal structure, using a vestibular instrument to assist in the process. The anatomy is favorable, facilitating the procedure.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the mesorectal attachments, working perpendicular to the rectum. After finishing the circumferential dissection, a stapler is prepared for use, typically with a blue load for a sigmoid resection.

The surgeon uses the first clamp to push out edema and fluid from the tissue before opening the stapler to accommodate more tissue safely. The stapler is clamped down fully before firing, ensuring complete closure.

The surgeon has divided the mesorectum and rectum, and is now identifying the proximal division point. The vessel sealer is introduced, and the sigmoid colon is rotated out of the pelvis. The descending sigmoid junction is tented and held to the anterior midline, preparing for the anastomotic site. The specimen is then flipped back into the pelvis.

The surgeon locates the divided inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle and adjusts the proximal division point, ensuring it remains with the specimen for lymph node harvesting. A visible lymph node is noted to the right of the IMA pedicle.
The surgeon begins double burning the mesentery using a vessel sealer, with the specimen and the IMA pedicle visible to the right. The procedure progresses from the IMA pedicle up to the third arm.
The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system to guide the vessel sealer, precisely dividing the mesentery while carefully detaching the final attachments.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to detach the final mesenteric attachments. After administering 2.5 cc of ICG with a 10 cc flush, the ICG is injected, causing the surgical field to illuminate, indicating good perfusion.

The anvil is inserted into the abdomen through an extended skin incision, requiring stretching of the fascia and peritoneum. The insertion process is delicate, especially in larger patients with deeper subcutaneous tissue.

The surgeon divides the descending sigmoid junction using either scissors or a vessel sealer, favoring the vessel sealer for a straighter cut. Three ovula are placed into the abdomen, and the surgeon begins suturing with a vessel sealer while waiting for the needle driver to arrive.

The surgeon begins suturing at the seven o'clock position on the descending colon conduit, using the third arm to hold the tissue approximately four centimeters proximal to the cuff. The needle driver is prepared to complete the suturing process.

The surgeon uses a needle driver to begin suturing at the seven o'clock position on the cuff of the descending colon conduit. The suturing is performed in a baseball stitch configuration, progressing in an 'into out' fashion around the conduit, with stitches placed approximately one centimeter apart.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand to take precise bites of tissue, traveling approximately one centimeter and taking half-centimeter bites each time. The instrument's width is five millimeters, and its opened jaw measures two centimeters, aiding in measurement accuracy. The suturing is performed in a clockwise fashion from the seven o'clock position back to the starting point.

The surgeon uses the third arm to hold the anvil at the twelve o'clock position while the left hand pulls down at the six o'clock position. The anvil is positioned, and the suture is cinched down tightly around the descending colon conduit. The surgeon may perform a second round of suturing if the cuff does not cinch down adequately.

The surgeon runs a second suture pass with the V-lock around the cuff of the descending colon conduit, without cutting the needle off. A vicryl endo loop is cinched tightly around the base of the anvil, securing the suture in place.

The surgeon runs the suture around the descending colon conduit a second time, keeping the needle attached for potential additional passes. Scissors are introduced to clean off the mesentery, and a right-angle instrument is used to perform electrocautery, mimicking the technique used in open cases for extracorporeal fixation of the anvils.

The surgeon exchanges the third arm of the robotic system to improve exposure, carefully thinning down the mesentery to achieve a symmetric and flat appearance. The mesentery is manipulated delicately to avoid superficial burns or devascularization while maintaining visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon ensures hemostasis and checks the orientation before cutting the suture. The specimen is placed in the right lower quadrant. The assistant begins transdental dilatation using EA sizers. The surgeon uses a Vestas instrument and checks the orientation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), which is visible.

The surgeon inserts an instrument near the splenic vein, revealing clear anatomical structures. The rectum is mobilized extensively, allowing for smooth extraction. The camera is cleaned to maintain visibility.
The assistant positions the EA sizers, followed by the insertion of the stapler. The surgeon operates at double speed, bringing the spike to one side of the rectal cuff.

The surgeon brings the transanal anvil spike through the left side of the rectal cuff, ensuring only one intersecting staple line point. The mesenteric orientation is checked to confirm proper alignment, with the intersecting staple line visible on the right-hand side of the anastomosis.
The E-A-Sizer is fired, and the assistant prepares to bring the colonoscope up. Saline is instilled into the pelvis, and the third arm clamps off to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon uses energy devices liberally in the surgical area, addressing any superficial bleeding with sutures if necessary. Indocyanine green (ICG) is reinjected, showing the rectum with good perfusion, which typically follows the perfusion of the sigmoid colon.
The endoscopic image on the left shows the anastomosis, which appears intact and without bleeding. The area is distending adequately, though not optimally.
The surgeon identifies a small thermal injury on the left side of the anastomosis and places an interrupted suture to cover it, using robotic assistance for precise manipulation.

The surgeon uses the vessel seater to place sutures, functioning as an off-label needle driver. The instrument is visible in action, though not typically used for delicate suturing tasks. The clamp is released, and the view switches back to full screen as the vessel seater is removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon switches the master controls on the robotic console, assigning arms number one and two to function as the left and right hands. The specimen is placed into a specimen bag and extracted through the extension of the staple port, completing the case.
